examples.title: Testing if Light Travels Straight
examples.content: Question: Does light travel in straight lines? Predict: Yes. Experiment: Shine a light through holes in paper. Watch the light. Analyze: The light makes straight lines through the holes. Conclude: Light does travel straight (in normal air). This is the scientific method in action.

examples.title: Why Does Salt Dissolve in Water?
examples.content: Question: Why does salt dissolve? Predict: Maybe water molecules pull the salt apart. Experiment: Mix salt and water, see it dissolve. Try with other liquids. Analyze: Salt dissolves in water but not oil. Conclude: Water has something special that salt likes. This leads to understanding polar molecules.

examples.title: Testing Gravity
examples.content: Question: Do all objects fall at the same speed? Predict: Maybe heavy things fall faster. Experiment: Drop heavy and light objects together. Watch them fall. Analyze: They hit the ground at the same time (ignoring air resistance). Conclude: Gravity affects all objects equally. This contradicted earlier predictions but that's okay—science learns!
